symptoms.symptom_name: Orthostatic tachycardia — heart rate rises markedly on standing and normalises supine

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A sustained >=30 bpm rise on standing that resolves supine is the defining feature of postural orthostatic tachycardia.
A strictly positional sinus tachycardia that normalises when lying flat is not the pattern of a fixed primary arrhythmia.

symptoms.symptom_name: Recurrent pre-syncope with visual greying on standing, relieved by lying down

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Orthostatic cerebral hypoperfusion producing pre-syncope relieved by recumbency is characteristic of orthostatic intolerance.

symptoms.symptom_name: Diffuse myofascial and joint pain in a known hypermobile patient

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Connective-tissue laxity with recurrent microtrauma/subluxation causes diffuse pain and clusters with the dysautonomia in hEDS flares.
Diffuse pain alone does not localise a dislocation — it neither confirms nor excludes one.
Objective hypermobility confirms the connective-tissue substrate for secondary dysautonomia — a clinician-measured sign, not obtainable over chat; the AI relies on the reported known diagnosis.

symptoms.symptom_name: Postural blood-pressure change on active stand (115/75 supine to 95/65 standing), supine BP preserved

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Preserved supine BP with a modest fall on standing accompanying a large HR rise fits orthostatic pooling rather than haemorrhagic hypovolaemia — clinician-measured, sealed.
A preserved supine blood pressure argues against significant blood loss causing the tachycardia.

symptoms.symptom_name: No melena or haematemesis; tachycardia positional and resolves supine

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of melena/haematemesis with positional, supine-resolving tachycardia is required to safely down-weight occult haemorrhage.

symptoms.symptom_name: Joints structurally located with preserved range of motion and intact peripheral neurovascular status

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Normal anatomical contours, preserved ROM and intact neurovascular status exclude an acute structural dislocation — clinician examination, sealed.

symptoms.symptom_name: Chronic history of identical recurrent flares of the same symptom cluster

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A recurrent, stereotyped, self-limiting flare pattern strongly favours the known chronic mechanism over a new acute emergency.
A chronic identical recurrent pattern is not how an acute occult haemorrhage behaves.
Normal oxygen saturation and no fever argue against a concurrent hypoxic or septic process but do not confirm the diagnosis; patient-reported oximetry, unverified.

symptoms.symptom_name: No completed syncope, no locked/deformed dislocation, no breathing difficulty at presentation

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A negative emergency screen keeps the presentation within the benign orthostatic-flare band and defines the safety-net triggers rather than the baseline tier.

symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: Upright heart racing + pre-syncope relieved supine + positional/supine-resolving pattern + a chronic recurrent identical history together make a benign secondary-dysautonomia flare highly probable and down-weight the emergency mimics — the gestalt is stronger than any single feature.

investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Reasonable outpatient confirmation to document sinus rhythm and exclude a primary arrhythmia — arranged via GP, not an emergency requirement given the positional, supine-resolving pattern.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Full blood count and iron studies
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Screens for anaemia/iron deficiency that can worsen fatigue and orthostatic intolerance; routine outpatient bloods.
The confirmatory test for POTS (sustained >=30 bpm HR rise on standing), arranged through the GP/dysautonomia service — not performable over chat.
Not indicated — there is no chest pain, no fixed tachycardia and a negative acute-red-flag screen; emergency cardiac/vascular imaging would be over-investigation of a recognised chronic flare.
